content.problemStatement:
Arun has a rooted tree of NNN vertices rooted at vertex 111. Each vertex can either be coloured black or white.

Initially, the vertices are coloured A1,A2,…ANA_1, A_2, \ldots A_NA1​,A2​,…AN​, where Ai∈{0,1}A_i \in \{0, 1\}Ai​∈{0,1} denotes the colour of the iii-th vertex (here 000 represents white and 111 represents black). He wants to perform some operations to change the colouring of the vertices to B1,B2,…BNB_1, B_2, \ldots B_NB1​,B2​,…BN​ respectively.

Arun can perform the following operation any number of times. In one operation, he can choose any subtree and either paint all its vertices white or all its vertices black.

Help Arun find the minimum number of operations required to change the colouring of the vertices to B1,B2,…BNB_1, B_2, \ldots B_NB1​,B2​,…BN​ respectively.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the tree.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the initial colouring of the vertices of the tree.
The third line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \dots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​ denoting the final desired colouring of the vertices of the tree.
The next N−1N-1N−1 lines contain two space-separated integers uuu and vvv — denoting an undirected edge between nodes uuu and vvv.

It is guaranteed that the edges given in the input form a tree.



Output Format
For each testcase, output the minimum number of operations required to obtain the desired colouring.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
1 ≤ u, v ≤ N, the given edges form a tree
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N, the number of vertices in the rooted tree (root is vertex 1).
- The second line contains N integers A1 … AN, where Ai is 0 (white) or 1 (black) – the initial colour of vertex i.
- The third line contains N integers B1 … BN, the desired colour of each vertex.
- The next N‑1 lines each contain two integers u and v, denoting an undirected edge of the tree.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of operations required to obtain the desired colouring.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: Paint the subtree rooted at vertex 3 black – one operation.
Test case 2: Paint subtree 3 black, then subtree 2 white – two operations. No solution with fewer operations exists.
Single vertex needs to change colour, so one painting operation is required.

solution.approach:
We process the tree with a depth‑first search starting from the root. While traversing we keep the colour that was last painted on the path from the root (‑1 means no paint yet). For a node v we can compute its current colour:
- If an ancestor subtree has been painted, the current colour is that painted colour.
- Otherwise it is the original colour A[v].
If the current colour differs from the required colour B[v] we must paint the whole subtree rooted at v to B[v]. This counts as one operation and the painted colour for all descendants becomes B[v]. Otherwise we inherit the colour from the nearest painted ancestor (or none). The greedy choice is optimal because any operation that does not fix the colour of the current node would be useless – the only way to change the colour of v is to paint a subtree that contains v, and the cheapest such subtree is the one rooted at v itself.
The algorithm therefore:
1. Build adjacency list.
2. Run DFS(root, parent, inheritedColour) where inheritedColour = -1 initially.
3. At each node compute effectiveColour = (inheritedColour == -1 ? A[node] : inheritedColour).
4. If effectiveColour != B[node] → answer++, newInherited = B[node]; else newInherited = inheritedColour.
5. Recurse to children with newInherited.
The total number of operations counted is minimal.
Complexities: O(N) time per test case, O(N) memory for the adjacency list and recursion stack.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of vertices of that test case. Over all test cases O( Σ N ) ≤ 3·10^5.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for adjacency lists and recursion stack.

code.Python3:
import sys
sys.setrecursionlimit(1_000_000)

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        A = [0] + [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        B = [0] + [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        adj = [[] for _ in range(N+1)]
        for _ in range(N-1):
            u = int(next(it)); v = int(next(it))
            adj[u].append(v)
            adj[v].append(u)
        ans = 0
        def dfs(u, parent, inherited):
            nonlocal ans
            cur = A[u] if inherited == -1 else inherited
            new_inherited = inherited
            if cur != B[u]:
                ans += 1
                new_inherited = B[u]
            for v in adj[u]:
                if v != parent:
                    dfs(v, u, new_inherited)
        dfs(1, 0, -1)
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
